[[1979年]][[2月3日]]に[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。

revision.text:
{{画像提供依頼||date=2006年11月}} '''住吉の御田植'''（すみよしのおたうえ）は、[[大阪府]][[大阪市]][[住吉区]]住吉の[[住吉大社]]に伝わる民俗芸能の[[田楽]]。
[[1979年]][[2月3日]]に[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。
== 関連項目 == *[[伝統芸能]] *[[田植|田植祭]] *[[巫女]] *[[稚児]] *[[厚化粧]] ==外聞リンク== *[http://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/calender/otaue.html 御田植え(住吉大社公式ウェブページ)]2009年9月22日(UTC)閲覧
{{japan-culture-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すみよしのおたうえ}} [[Category:大阪市の歴史]] [[Category:住吉区]] [[Category:大阪府の祭り]] [[Category:重要無形民俗文化財]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|住吉神社御田植神事。]]{{画像提供依頼||date=2006年11月|cat=大阪市}} '''住吉の御田植'''（すみよしのおたうえ）は、[[大阪府]][[大阪市]][[住吉区]]住吉の[[住吉大社]]に伝わる民俗芸能の[[田楽]]（[[御田植祭]]）である。
[[稲作]]の[[歴史]]始まって以来、[[田の神]]をまつる[[田植]]行事は日本の全国各地で行われているが、なかでも当社の[[御田]]は[[神功皇后]]が[[水田]]を設けたことに始まるとされ、[[五穀豊穣]]に関わる神聖な[[行事]]が現在でも厳格に[[伝承]]されている。母なる[[大地]]に植られた[[イネ]]の苗には強力な穀霊が宿り、[[田植え]]に際して[[音楽]]を奏で[[歌]]を歌い[[踊り]]や[[舞]]を演じるのは、水田や植えられる苗に宿る[[穀物]]の力を増すためのものと考えられ、穀物が豊かに育ち[[稲穂]]が十分に実る[[秋]]を迎えるための[[儀式]]として、おごそかに執り行われている。
この御田植は、現在は[[6月14日]]に催され、当日の午前、神館で植女や[[稚児]]、御稔女らが[[衣装]]を改め[[化粧]]を施し[[神酒]]をいただく粉黛式と戴盃式が行われる。ついで[[日本三舞台|石舞台]]において[[神事]]に奉仕する全員が[[修祓]]を受け、第一本宮で神事の奉告祭を行い、楽人、[[八乙女]]、御稔女、植女、替植女、稚児、風流武者、[[住吉踊り]]の踊子などが行列を整えて御田へ向かう。その後、御神水を御田の四方に注ぎ清め、[[早苗]]の授受が行なわれる。植女から替植女に早苗が渡されると田植えがはじまり、その間、[[舞台]]と田の[[畔]]とで八乙女による田舞、風流武者が[[紅白]]に分かれ、陣鐘・[[太鼓]]・[[ほら貝]]を鳴らしながら[[六尺棒]]を打ち合う棒打合戦、長柄の[[傘]]を開いて持つ音頭取の歌につれ、[[菅笠]]をつけた僧形の[[子供]]たちが、[[団扇]]を打ちながら傘のまわりを踊りまわる住吉踊など、様々な[[芸能]]が[[奉納]]される。
[[1979年]]（[[昭和]]54年）[[2月3日]]に[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。
== 関連項目 == * [[伝統芸能]]
==外部リンク== *[http://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/calender/otaue.html 御田植え(住吉大社公式ウェブページ)]2009年9月22日(UTC)閲覧 *{{国指定文化財等データベース|302|103|住吉の御田植}}
{{Coord|34|36|40.86|N|135|29|32.83|E|region:JP-27_type:event_scale:10000|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すみよしのおたうえ}} [[Category:住吉区の歴史]] [[Category:大阪府の祭り]] [[Category:重要無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:住吉大社]] {{japan-culture-stub}}

[[1979年]]（[[昭和]]54年）[[2月3日]]に[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。

revision.comment: 曖昧さ回避ページ[[行事]]へのリンクを解消、リンク先を[[イベント]]に変更([[User:ネイ/DisamAssist|DisamAssist]]使用)
revision.text:
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|住吉大社御田植神事。]] '''住吉の御田植'''（すみよしのおたうえ）は、[[大阪府]][[大阪市]][[住吉区]]住吉の[[住吉大社]]に伝わる民俗芸能の[[田楽]]（[[御田植祭]]）である。
[[稲作]]の[[歴史]]始まって以来、[[田の神]]をまつる[[田植]]行事は日本の全国各地で行われているが、なかでも当社の[[御田]]は[[神功皇后]]が[[水田]]を設けたことに始まるとされ、[[五穀豊穣]]に関わる神聖な[[イベント|行事]]が現在でも厳格に[[伝承]]されている。母なる[[大地]]に植られた[[イネ]]の苗には強力な穀霊が宿り、[[田植え]]に際して[[音楽]]を奏で[[歌]]を歌い[[踊り]]や[[舞]]を演じるのは、水田や植えられる苗に宿る[[穀物]]の力を増すためのものと考えられ、穀物が豊かに育ち[[稲穂]]が十分に実る[[秋]]を迎えるための[[儀式]]として、おごそかに執り行われている。
この御田植は、現在は[[6月14日]]に催され、当日の午前、神館で植女や[[稚児]]、御稔女らが[[衣装]]を改め[[化粧]]を施し[[神酒]]をいただく粉黛式と戴盃式が行われる。ついで[[日本三舞台|石舞台]]において[[神事]]に奉仕する全員が[[修祓]]を受け、第一本宮で神事の奉告祭を行い、楽人、[[八乙女]]、御稔女、植女、替植女、稚児、風流武者、[[住吉踊り]]の踊子などが行列を整えて御田へ向かう。その後、御神水を御田の四方に注ぎ清め、[[早苗]]の授受が行なわれる。植女から替植女に早苗が渡されると田植えがはじまり、その間、[[舞台]]と田の[[畔]]とで八乙女による田舞、風流武者が[[紅白]]に分かれ、陣鐘・[[太鼓]]・[[ほら貝]]を鳴らしながら[[六尺棒]]を打ち合う棒打合戦、長柄の[[傘]]を開いて持つ音頭取の歌につれ、[[菅笠]]をつけた僧形の[[子供]]たちが、[[団扇]]を打ちながら傘のまわりを踊りまわる住吉踊など、様々な[[芸能]]が[[奉納]]される。
[[1979年]]（[[昭和]]54年）[[2月3日]]に[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。
== 関連項目 == * [[伝統芸能]]
==外部リンク== *[https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 御田植え(住吉大社公式ウェブページ)]2009年9月22日(UTC)閲覧 *{{国指定文化財等データベース|302|103|住吉の御田植}}

revision.text:
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|god of the rice field|ja|田の神}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bountiful harvests|ja|五穀豊穣}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|children attendants|ja|稚児}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|conch shells|ja|ほら貝}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|god of the rice field|ja|田の神}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bountiful harvests|ja|五穀豊穣}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|children attendants|ja|稚児}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.

revision.text:
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|god of the rice field|ja|田の神}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bountiful harvests|ja|五穀豊穣}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|children attendants|ja|稚児}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|conch shells|ja|ほら貝}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}

On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|god of the rice field|ja|田の神}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bountiful harvests|ja|五穀豊穣}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|children attendants|ja|稚児}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|conch shells|ja|ほら貝}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|god of the rice field|ja|田の神}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bountiful harvests|ja|五穀豊穣}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|children attendants|ja|稚児}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|conch shells|ja|ほら貝}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pages needing attribution]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植}}

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. [[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

revision.text:
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.

Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. [[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en}}
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植}}

revision.text:
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|ja|田の神|Ta-no-Kami=Ta-no-Kami|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|bumper crop|ja|五穀豊穣|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|ja|6月14日}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo |ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979年2月3日}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}}.
