<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Otaue Shinji]][[ja:住吉の御田植]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11381803]]
Wikidata name: Otaue Shinto Service
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]]
'''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}} at [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] in [[:en:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]], Osaka, Japan.

Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice cultivation|1=rice farming|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q734080}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|god of the rice field]] have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to [[:en:Bumper crop|bountiful harvests]]. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earth|1=Earth|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10934375}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.

This Otaue ritual is held annually on [[:en:June 14|June 14]]. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo|ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chigo|qq=}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Three stages of Japan|lt=Three stages of Japan|lt=Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Stage|qq=}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yaotome|qq=}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Odori|1=Sumiyoshi Odori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20045159}}, then heads to the rice field.

After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Charonia tritonis|1=Charonia tritonis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16767561}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.

On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979th year 2月3日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 3, 1979|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}.

The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979th year 2月3日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 3, 1979|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Otaue Shinji]][[ja:住吉の御田植]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11381803]] Wikidata name: Otaue Shinto Service [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}} at [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] in [[:en:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]], Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice cultivation|1=rice farming|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q734080}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|god of the rice field]] have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to [[:en:Bumper crop|bountiful harvests]]. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earth|1=Earth|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10934375}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on [[:en:June 14|June 14]]. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo|ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chigo|qq=}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Three stages of Japan|lt=Three stages of Japan|lt=Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Stage|qq=}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yaotome|qq=}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Odori|1=Sumiyoshi Odori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20045159}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Charonia tritonis|1=Charonia tritonis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16767561}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979th year 2月3日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 3, 1979|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]]
'''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}} at [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] in [[:en:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]], Osaka, Japan.

Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice cultivation|1=rice farming|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q734080}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|god of the rice field]] have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to [[:en:Bumper crop|bountiful harvests]]. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earth|1=Earth|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10934375}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.

This Otaue ritual is held annually on [[:en:June 14|June 14]]. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo|ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chigo|qq=}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Three stages of Japan|lt=Three stages of Japan|lt=Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Stage|qq=}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yaotome|qq=}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Odori|1=Sumiyoshi Odori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20045159}}, then heads to the rice field.

After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Charonia tritonis|1=Charonia tritonis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16767561}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.

On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979th year 2月3日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 3, 1979|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}.

It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earth|1=Earth|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10934375}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on [[:en:June 14|June 14]]. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo|ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chigo|qq=}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Three stages of Japan|lt=Three stages of Japan|lt=Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Stage|qq=}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yaotome|qq=}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Odori|1=Sumiyoshi Odori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20045159}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Charonia tritonis|1=Charonia tritonis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16767561}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979th year 2月3日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 3, 1979|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}

Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to [[:en:Bumper crop|bountiful harvests]]. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo|ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chigo|qq=}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yaotome|qq=}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Odori|1=Sumiyoshi Odori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20045159}}, then heads to the rice field.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Otaue Shinji]][[ja:住吉の御田植]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Otaue Shinji]][[ja:住吉の御田植]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Otaue Shinji]][[ja:住吉の御田植]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11381803]] Wikidata name: Otaue Shinto Service [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}} at [[:en:Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]] in [[:en:Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka]], Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice cultivation|1=rice farming|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q734080}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the [[:en:Ta-no-Kami|god of the rice field]] have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to [[:en:Bumper crop|bountiful harvests]]. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earth|1=Earth|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10934375}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on [[:en:June 14|June 14]]. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo|ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chigo|qq=}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Three stages of Japan|lt=Three stages of Japan|lt=Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Stage|qq=}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yaotome|qq=}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Odori|1=Sumiyoshi Odori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20045159}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Charonia tritonis|1=Charonia tritonis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16767561}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979th year 2月3日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 3, 1979|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}

Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Three stages of Japan|lt=Three stages of Japan|lt=Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Stage|qq=}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yaotome|qq=}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Odori|1=Sumiyoshi Odori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20045159}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Charonia tritonis|1=Charonia tritonis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16767561}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979th year 2月3日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 3, 1979|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|en|Sumiyoshi-taisha|WD=Q705949|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|en|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|WD=Q985702|lt=Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice cultivation|1=rice farming|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q734080}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|en|Ta-no-Kami|WD=Q7683716|lt=god of the rice field}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|Bumper crop|en|Bumper crop|WD=Q4997332|lt=bountiful harvests}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earth|1=Earth|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10934375}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|en|June 14|WD=Q2612|lt=June 14}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo|ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chigo|qq=}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Three stages of Japan|lt=Three stages of Japan|lt=Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Stage|qq=}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yaotome|qq=}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Odori|1=Sumiyoshi Odori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20045159}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Charonia tritonis|1=Charonia tritonis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16767561}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979th year 2月3日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 3, 1979|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Otaue Shinji]][[ja:住吉の御田植]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Otaue Shinji]][[ja:住吉の御田植]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Otaue Shinji]][[ja:住吉の御田植]]
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Festival|lt=Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otaue Matsuri|qq=}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|en|Sumiyoshi-taisha|WD=Q705949|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|en|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|WD=Q985702|lt=Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|1=Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka}}, Osaka, Japan.
Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice cultivation|1=Rice Cultivation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q734080}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|en|Ta-no-Kami|WD=Q7683716|lt=god of the rice field|1=Ta-no-Kami}} have been held throughout the country. Among them, Sumiyoshi Taisha's Otaue is believed to have originated from {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} establishing a rice field. This sacred event, which remains faithfully preserved today, is dedicated to {{ill|Bumper crop|en|Bumper crop|WD=Q4997332|lt=bountiful harvests|1=Bumper crop}}. It is believed that strong spirits reside in the rice seedlings planted in the mother {{ill|Earth|ja|大地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earth|1=Q10934375|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10934375}}. During the planting, music, songs, dances, and performances are offered to enhance the vitality of the grains, ensuring their rich growth and plentiful yield in the autumn.
This Otaue ritual is held annually on {{ill|June 14|en|June 14|WD=Q2612|lt=June 14|1=June 14}}. On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo|ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chigo|qq=}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Three stages of Japan|lt=Three stages of Japan|lt=Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Stage|qq=}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yaotome|qq=}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Odori|1=Sumiyoshi Odori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20045159}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Charonia tritonis|1=conch shells|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16767561}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979th year 2月3日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 3, 1979|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=Theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植}}
{{Coord|34|36|40.86|N|135|29|32.83|E|region:JP-27_type:event_scale:10000|display=title}}
{{translated page|ja|住吉の御田植|version=104313113|comment=Imported full ja history}}

On the morning of the event, ceremonies called Powder Ceremony (粉黛式) and Sake Offering Ceremony (戴盃式) take place at the shrine's inner sanctum, where planters, {{ill|chigo|ja|稚児|chigo=chigo|lt=children attendants|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/chigo|qq=}}, and ceremonial women (御稔女) don special attire, apply makeup, and partake in sacred sake. Following this, all participants undergo purification (修祓) at the {{ill|Three stages of Japan|lt=Three stages of Japan|lt=Stone Stage|ja|日本三舞台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Stage|qq=}} and attend a dedication ceremony at the First Shrine (第一本宮). The procession, including musicians, {{ill|yaotome|ja|八乙女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yaotome|qq=}}, ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|ja|住吉踊り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi Odori|1=Sumiyoshi Odori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20045159}}, then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|Charonia tritonis|ja|ほら貝|lt=conch shells|Charonia tritonis=Charonia tritonis|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Charonia tritonis|1=conch shells|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16767561}}, and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|ja|1979th year 2月3日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 3, 1979|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|ja|伝統芸能|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の伝統芸能|1=Theatre of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1364447}}
